There are a number of standards of service, but what comes to mind at short notice are three in particular in terms of metrics so we have our mean time to repair and what that looks at is when you have a fault in your service how long do we take to fix it we have the mean time to install once you come into a play for a service with us how long does it take for us to get that service installed and working and then we have our nps score which looks at how satisfied you are with our business and with the service that we're delivering. And we speak with Xavier Jeffrey, manager of Customer Services at C&W Business. Xavier, it's always great to connect with you, and even more special this time, as you've recently made a transition within the company to Manager of Customer Services on the B2B side. But what that means is that you're perfectly positioned to help us continue our conversation about the Fair Trading Commission's Standards of Service. Now, can you please tell us what the Standards of Service mean to the company, its customers, and of course, the local telecommunications industry on the whole? Thanks, Alan. The Standards of Service really represent a framework that outlines the minimum standards for quality, customer service, and reliability that we're supposed to meet our customers. So these standards of service really allow our customers to have an expectation and to hold us accountable in the event that the expectation is not met. And I know the Fair Trading Commission's standards relate only to fixed line services, but the company has adopted a policy where they've extended those standards for their entire portfolio of products and services. Yeah, so we're absolutely committed and dedicated to being able to deliver service to our customers at a very high level. And as a result of that, it would be irresponsible for us to only look at fixed line services in how we deliver these services. So what we've done is we've taken these standards and we've decided to apply it to every line of business so that at a minimum, customers know what to expect. And as a business, we can say to our customers, this is what good looks like. So how do you track these metrics? Do you use any tools, so to speak? There are a number of standards of service, but what comes to mind at short notice are three in particular in terms of metrics so we have our mean time to repair and what that looks at is when you have a fault in your service how long do we take to fix it we have the mean time to install once you come into a play for a service with us how long does it take for us to get that service installed and working and then we have our nps score which looks at how satisfied you are with our business and with the service that we're delivering. And what we've seen is that there's been a major improvement over the last year for all of those metrics across the board. We know that in the past, we messed up, but as a business, we've taken a conscious decision to fix that. So we're not at the end of the lane as yet, but we're working towards improving it and we can see the improvement and that's not just from a local perspective because you were telling me that it's across the caribbean wide for flow correct so as you may know that we have contact centers in jamaica and trinidad and as a business like i said we're dedicated to this promise to deliver excellent customer service to our customers so we're required and we really have to do this at all channels so whether our customer comes into a retail store whether they visit our customer experience center at winsor lodge or even call into one of our contact centers we have to make sure that the level of service that they receive across the board is standard and is of excellent quality xavier thank you so very much that was x Xavier Jeffrey, Manager of Customer Services at C&W Business.
